sections.heading: What is Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE)?
sections.content: Your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE) is the total number of calories your body burns each day, including all activities — from resting and sleeping to walking, working, and exercising. Knowing your TDEE is essential for effective weight management, muscle gain, and overall fitness planning.

sections.heading: How to Calculate TDEE
sections.content: Using our TDEE Calculator Online is simple and accurate:

subSections.heading: Step 1
subSections.content: Enter your age, gender, weight, and height.

subSections.heading: Step 2
subSections.content: Select your activity level (Sedentary, Lightly Active, Moderately Active, Very Active, Extra Active).

subSections.heading: Step 3
subSections.content: Optionally, input your body fat % for more precise results.

subSections.heading: Step 4
subSections.content: Click 'Calculate TDEE' to instantly get your daily calorie needs.

subSections.heading: Mifflin-St Jeor Formula
subSections.content: Best for most users, estimates TDEE using age, gender, height, and weight.

subSections.heading: Katch-McArdle Formula
subSections.content: Ideal if you know your body fat percentage, as it calculates TDEE based on Lean Body Mass.

sections.heading: Activity Multipliers
sections.content: After calculating Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR), the calculator applies an activity multiplier:

subSections.heading: Weight Loss
subSections.content: Eat 10–20% fewer calories than your TDEE.

subSections.heading: Maintenance
subSections.content: Consume calories equal to your TDEE.

subSections.heading: Muscle Gain
subSections.content: Eat 10–20% above TDEE with strength training.

sections.heading: TDEE vs BMR
sections.content: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the calories burned at rest, while TDEE includes all activity and exercise. Knowing both helps fine-tune your diet and training plan.

sections.heading: Tips for Accuracy
sections.content: Track progress over time and update TDEE as your weight, activity, or body composition changes. Pair TDEE tracking with macronutrient monitoring for best results.

faq.q: What is TDEE and why is it important?
faq.a: TDEE is the total calories your body burns daily. Knowing it helps plan for weight loss, maintenance, or muscle gain.

faq.q: What’s the difference between TDEE and BMR?
faq.a: BMR is calories burned at rest, while TDEE includes calories burned from activity and exercise.

faq.q: Which formula is better: Mifflin-St Jeor or Katch-McArdle?
faq.a: Mifflin-St Jeor works best for most people. Katch-McArdle is more accurate if you know your body fat percentage.

faq.q: How many calories should I eat to lose weight?
faq.a: Aim for 10–20% below your TDEE for safe and sustainable fat loss.
Yes, it changes with weight, body composition, activity level, and metabolism.

faq.q: Is this TDEE Calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes, our Online TDEE Calculator Free is available anytime without cost.

faq.q: How can I use TDEE for muscle gain?
faq.a: Eat 10–20% above TDEE with strength training for lean muscle growth.

faq.q: Which activity multiplier should I choose?
faq.a: Choose the one closest to your daily lifestyle and workout routine for best accuracy.

faq.q: How accurate is a TDEE Calculator?
faq.a: It provides estimates. Accuracy improves if you include body fat percentage and track real progress.
